Doi marinari sunt în continuare consideraţi dispăruţi în urma acestui eveniment.

medical.title: Colorectal cancer research reveals new mechanism in targeted therapy against metastasis
medical.summary: Researchers at the Medical University of Vienna have described a mechanism in metastatic colorectal cancer that has received little attention to date: A key target of existing therapies, the so-called epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), is likely to affect more than just the cancer cells themselves. The study shows that EGFR also influences certain immune cells in the tumor microenvironment—and thereby helps determine how effectively the body's own defenses can combat the tumor. The findings have recently been published in the journal Cell Death & Differentiation.

medical.title: Physical and mental capacity linked to disability before death
medical.summary: For many older adults, aging well means remaining independent—being able to do everyday activities like buying groceries or cooking dinner without help. A new Yale School of Medicine study suggests that changes in mental, physical or sensory capacity may be linked to a person's future ability to perform these routine tasks. The study is published in The Lancet Healthy Longevity.

medical.title: With neuronal data, AI models predict grammar, meaning and context of spoken sentences
medical.summary: By applying machine-learning models to single-cell brain recordings taken from humans in conversation, a research team identified both individual and collective neuronal activity that reflected key features of language. The work, published in Nature, offers unprecedented insight into how neurons encode linguistic information, suggesting that brain activity may one day be used to infer speech-related thoughts, which could be transformative for some patients.

medical.title: Rewired metabolism helps revive exhausted immune cells and boost cancer immunity
medical.summary: Researchers from National Taiwan University (NTU) and National Taiwan University Hospital (NTUH) have identified a promising way to reinvigorate the body's cancer-fighting immune cells by rewiring their metabolism, revealing a potential new strategy for cancer immunotherapy.

medical.title: When health matters, status doesn't
medical.summary: People become much less concerned about being better off than other people when health enters the picture, according to new research.

medical.title: Neighborhood opportunities can shape children's brain development, study finds
medical.summary: The environment in which children grow up can play a central role in their mental development and psychological well-being. For instance, past studies have found that a family's socioeconomic status and the opportunities available to children are linked to different developmental outcomes, such as learning trajectories, attention and problem-solving capabilities.

medical.title: Psilocybin combined with psychotherapy demonstrates antidepressant effects over a 12-month period
medical.summary: Psilocybin combined with psychotherapy can lead to long-term improvements in depressive symptoms in people with treatment-resistant depression. This has been shown by a recently published long-term follow-up study conducted by the Central Institute of Mental Health (CIMH), Charité–Universitätsmedizin Berlin and the MIND Foundation. The antidepressant effects persisted for up to 12 months.

medical.title: Lymphatic dysfunction drives heart valve disease in Marfan syndrome patients
medical.summary: Dysfunction in the lymphatic system has been identified as a hidden driver of life-threatening heart valve disease in patients with Marfan syndrome, according to a new Northwestern Medicine study published in the Journal of Clinical Investigation.

medical.title: Premature babies—a simple test gauges neurocognitive development
medical.summary: Canadian scientists have demonstrated for the first time that a noninvasive brain-monitoring technique could help medical teams predict the future development of premature babies.

medical.title: AI tool outperforms existing methods for identifying severe childhood pneumonia
medical.summary: Pneumonia remains the leading infectious cause of death among children under 5 worldwide, claiming almost 1 million lives each year. Researchers at University College Dublin have developed an artificial intelligence tool that can more accurately identify young children with pneumonia at serious risk of needing hospital treatment. Found to significantly outperform existing risk assessment methods used to identify children who require urgent referral to the hospital, the BIOTOPE algorithm has the potential to save lives in low-resource health care settings.

medical.title: Infant sleep movements reveal 60-minute cycles of inactivity that lengthen through first year
medical.summary: Infants' activity while sleeping is rhythmic, according to a new study from the University of Surrey. The findings provide crucial insight into sleep cycles of infants in the first year of life. In the largest study of its kind, scientists from Surrey, led by Dr. Eva Winnebeck, investigated the sleep cycle development of 152 infants at ages 3, 6 and 12 months.

medical.title: How early life experiences shape schizophrenia risk
medical.summary: Carnegie Mellon University and University of California, San Francisco researchers have found that childhood trauma, poverty, social isolation and other adverse life experiences are associated with brain changes linked to schizophrenia-spectrum disorders—findings that could help researchers identify people at risk earlier and develop interventions before severe symptoms emerge.

medical.title: Everyday chemical exposures linked to preterm birth and lower birthweight
medical.summary: In one of the largest studies of chemical exposures during pregnancy to date, new research led by the UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health, Stanford University School of Medicine and Woods Institute for the Environment has found that pregnant women are exposed to dozens of everyday chemicals, many of which can affect how early a child is born or a child's weight at birth. These outcomes influence a child's health throughout life.

medical.title: Birth control pills may increase binge eating, new study reveals
medical.summary: A new study from Michigan State University found increases in binge eating when taking hormone pills in the form of oral contraceptives—but not in all women. This is the first large-scale study of changes in binge-related symptoms with oral contraceptive use.

medical.title: Newborn neurons routinely break then repair DNA during brain cortex formation, study reveals
medical.summary: Newborn nerve cells must squeeze through crowded, narrow spaces—through dense tissue, past other cells, and between fibers—to reach the areas where they form neural circuits in the brain cortex. In a study published in Nature, researchers at Kyoto University's Institute for Integrated Cell-Material Sciences (WPI-iCeMS) and their collaborators report that this journey causes widespread DNA damage in neurons, resulting in double-strand breaks in which both strands of the double helix are completely severed.

medical.title: Parents of newborn girls are more likely to refuse lifesaving vitamin K and hepatitis B vaccine shot, researchers find
medical.summary: A simple shot given shortly after birth can protect babies from a rare but potentially life-threatening condition known as vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB). When newborns don't have enough vitamin K, their blood can't clot effectively, putting them at risk of severe internal bleeding in the intestines or the brain, which can cause organ damage or even prove fatal.

medical.title: How do beta blocker heart drugs actually work for anxiety?
medical.summary: A reality TV star's claim that a beta blocker dulled their emotions and made them appear calm on camera has sparked a wave of social media commentary.

medical.title: Ozempic and Wegovy linked to surprising drop in violent behavior
medical.summary: A Rutgers study suggests GLP-1 drugs such as Ozempic and Wegovy may weaken the link between impulsive tendencies and violent behavior. The surprising finding hints that these medications could affect how people act on impulses, though researchers stress that cause and effect have not been proven.

medical.title: These tiny holes could change how the world cleans water
medical.summary: A new nature-inspired membrane uses perfectly uniform one-nanometer pores to filter molecules with remarkable precision. The technology could transform industries such as pharmaceuticals and textiles by reducing energy consumption, improving water reuse, and delivering separation performance far beyond current filters.

medical.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
medical.summary: A low-calorie fasting-style diet significantly reduced inflammation linked to gum disease in a small clinical study. The findings suggest that what people eat may influence gum health almost as much as what they do with a toothbrush.

medical.title: Scientists discover a surprising cancer link to Alzheimer’s disease
medical.summary: Researchers discovered that mutations linked to blood cancers may help trigger Alzheimer’s disease by creating overly inflammatory immune cells in the brain. The unexpected finding could lead to new blood-based screening methods and potential treatments borrowed from cancer medicine.

medical.title: Stanford scientists regrow lost cartilage and reverse arthritis in major breakthrough
medical.summary: A new treatment that blocks an aging-related protein restored lost cartilage in old mice and helped prevent arthritis after knee injuries. Human cartilage samples showed similar signs of regeneration, raising hopes for a future drug that could repair joints instead of replacing them.

science.title: Swiss lake symbiosis reveals unexpected role in nitrogen cycling
science.summary: A publication led by researchers from the Max Planck Institute for Marine Microbiology in Bremen, Germany, shows that microscopic partnerships between ciliates and bacteria play a role in the nitrogen cycle of lakes. The study, published in The ISME Journal, investigates what determines the ecological niche of the remarkable symbiosis, and how strongly the host depends on its microbial partners.

science.title: 'High-res' is the secret to finding alien life with the next great space telescope
science.summary: We're still in the definition phase of the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO), but it seems like every week a new research group comes out with a paper helping to shape what is becoming one of the most important space telescopes of the 2040s. A new paper posted to the arXiv preprint server from a team of researchers led by Daniel Jaffe of the University of Texas at Austin contributes to this ongoing definition work by arguing that it's time HWO adopted a high-resolution near-IR spectroscopy capability—which sounds great in practice, but so far hasn't been attempted because of technological limitations. But, according to the paper, two recent inventions finally make a working version of an extremely high-resolution exoplanet hunter viable.

science.title: Bringing ancient light-sensing proteins back to life
science.summary: Resurrecting dinosaurs using DNA retrieved from a mosquito trapped in amber is a great movie plot, though it's less likely to happen in the real world. However, researchers have been trying to unlock the secrets behind the evolution of a single protein family to understand the evolution of ancestral proteins.

science.title: Is the customer always right? Study finds rude customers hurt business
science.summary: Frontline employees who face rude or disrespectful customers are more likely to justify negative behaviors, from cutting corners to leaving their jobs, according to a new study.

science.title: Reversible chirality switching in MoS₂ generates spin currents without magnets
science.summary: A newly developed method allows researchers to dynamically switch chirality—a particular lack of mirror symmetry—to generate spin currents in semiconductors, researchers from Science Tokyo report. Their approach relies on the reversible insertion and removal of small chiral molecules from the interlayer gaps of a layered, nonchiral semiconductor material using electrochemistry.

science.title: Cockroach genomes are packed with DNA transferred by their endosymbiont bacterial partners
science.summary: Genes aren't just transferred from parents to their offspring. Nature has found other ways to pass on genetic information, even between different species. And a new study, published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, reports that cockroaches, in particular, are riddled with DNA transferred from another species.

science.title: Skin and color pattern of 125-million-year-old crocodile revealed by extraordinary fossil from the Pyrenees
science.summary: A new study published in Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society describes, for the first time in detail, the soft tissues preserved in Montsecosuchus depereti, a Lower Cretaceous crocodylomorph from the Pedrera de Meià site in the province of Lleida (Catalonia, Spain). The analysis, based on ultraviolet light, provides new clues to the coloration of these animals, as well as the evolution of the respiratory system. The research was led by the Institut Català de Paleontologia Miquel Crusafont.

science.title: Global data declaration targets illegal fishing
science.summary: Fifteen countries signed a global declaration Wednesday aimed at combating illegal and destructive fishing with better data.

science.title: Drug-free nanoparticles stop tumor growth by transmitting biological messages to immune cells
science.summary: A research team from the Technion's Wolfson Faculty of Chemical Engineering has developed an original technology for treating cancer using nanoparticles that carry no drugs at all and has demonstrated its effectiveness against particularly dangerous and stubborn tumors.

science.title: Semiconductor chip writes 64 DNA sequences in water, setting new enzymatic benchmark
science.summary: Silicon chips have powered computing for half a century. Increasingly, they are also becoming platforms to read and manipulate biology at scale—recording from many neurons, reading many DNA sequences and now synthesizing DNA.

science.title: New heat-regulating fabric feels fluffy like cotton—but doesn't get wet
science.summary: Once cotton gets wet, it pulls heat from your body. This is helpful when you're exercising or outside on a hot day, but dangerous in the bitter cold. Now, researchers reporting in ACS Energy Letters have created an ultralight synthetic fiber material with cotton-like fluffiness that also repels water. The prototype fabric outperformed regular cotton in both retaining heat in the cold and releasing heat at room temperature.

science.title: 800 visits to a suburban park: Inside an epic 40‑year birdwatching quest
science.summary: Birdwatching is one of the most common and popular ways people experience nature. But it's not often that even the most enthusiastic birdwatcher returns to the same place more than 800 times to witness the changes over four decades.

science.title: Quantum sensor overcomes major obstacle in search for dark matter and gravitational waves
science.summary: A prototype quantum sensor developed by researchers at Imperial has demonstrated for the first time that a key principle behind next-generation quantum detectors can work under realistic conditions.

science.title: Thawing permafrost may trigger overlooked carbon sink in rivers
science.summary: A new study published in Nature shows that rock weathering increasingly counteracts river CO2 emissions as permafrost degrades. The study was carried out by a collaborative team of researchers from Umeå University in Sweden and East China Normal University.

science.title: Bees avoid too much of a good thing by balancing nutrients in pollen, study reveals
science.summary: New Oxford University-led research reveals that bees can regulate their feeding to avoid overconsuming certain essential nutrients, and that honey bees make a specialist "baby food" that gives their larvae a better-balanced diet.

science.title: Mountain lions have major ecological impact even in small preserves
science.summary: Big cats have a big impact. A long-term study showed that when mountain lions began regular visits to a small suburban preserve about 45 miles (72 kilometers) south of San Francisco, they changed the behavior of many other animals.

science.title: Levels of 'forever chemicals' in dolphins and whales are rising globally
science.summary: Whales and dolphins inhabit some of the largest and seemingly most pristine environments on Earth, from tropical coastlines to Antarctic waters. Yet even they cannot escape PFAS—persistent "forever chemicals" that leak from our homes, factories and waterways into the sea.

science.title: Scientists discover spider that disguises itself as a parasitic fungus
science.summary: Scientists have discovered a new Amazonian spider with an astonishing disguise: it looks like a parasitic fungus. The species, Taczanowskia waska, mimics both the appearance and behavior of the fungus, helping it stay hidden from predators and potentially catch prey more easily.

science.title: On the brink of extinction, the vaquita gets a digital lifeline
science.summary: Scientists have digitally preserved the world’s most endangered marine mammal by creating highly detailed 3D models of a vaquita skeleton using advanced imaging technology. The virtual archive provides an unprecedented look at the species and could help inspire conservation efforts before the tiny porpoise disappears forever.

science.title: Giant underground neutrino detector brings scientists closer to cracking the neutrino puzzle
science.summary: Deep beneath the ground in China, the massive JUNO neutrino observatory has delivered its first major scientific breakthrough, achieving one of the most precise measurements yet of how elusive neutrinos change as they travel. Using just 59 days of data, researchers sharply improved measurements of key neutrino properties, boosting confidence that JUNO can tackle one of particle physics' biggest mysteries: determining the true mass hierarchy of neutrinos.

science.title: The missing notebooks that solved a 55-million-year-old fossil mystery
science.summary: A spectacular fossil fish discovered on a remote cliff in New Zealand nearly 30 years ago has finally revealed its full story thanks to an unexpected discovery: the original collector’s long-lost field notebooks. The 1.2-meter fossil, preserved in stunning three-dimensional detail, belonged to an ancient tarpon-like predator that cruised New Zealand waters about 55 million years ago.

science.title: A legendary golden fabric lost for 2,000 years has returned
science.summary: Researchers in South Korea have recreated the legendary “sea silk” once prized by emperors, using fibers from a clam cultivated in Korean coastal waters. They discovered that its famous golden shine comes from tiny protein structures that reflect light rather than from pigments or dyes. Because the color is built into the fiber’s structure, it can remain vibrant for centuries.

science.title: Ancient DNA shared with Neanderthals may explain human language
science.summary: A tiny set of ancient genetic “switches” may have played a surprisingly large role in making human language possible. Researchers found that these DNA regions, which act like volume controls for genes involved in brain development, have an outsized influence on language ability despite making up less than 0.1% of the genome.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Felix Folk Festival 2026: Băile Felix devine, pentru două seri, capitala muzicii folk, a culturii și a întâlnirilor literare
mix_items.summary: Stațiunea Băile Felix va găzdui, în perioada 20–21 iunie 2026, ediția a IX-a a Festivalului Național de Muzică Folk „Felix Folk Festival”, un eveniment dedicat muzicii live, poeziei, literaturii și… Articolul Felix Folk Festival 2026: Băile Felix devine, pentru două seri, capitala muzicii folk, a culturii și a întâlnirilor literare apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Portugalia a devenit favorita Mondialului! Ronaldo e blocat la Casa de Pensii și nu joacă
mix_items.summary: Veste extraordinară primită de Naționala Portugaliei cu câteva ore înainte de debutul la Cupa Mondială. Marele atârnache al echipei, Cristiano Ronaldo, este blocat la o coadă la Casa de Pensii, unde încearcă să-și actualizeze dosarul, astfel că nu va juca în mod cert în partida de diseară cu Congo. La viteza cu care se mișcă […] The post Portugalia a devenit favorita Mondialului! Ronaldo e blocat la Casa de Pensii și nu joacă appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Casa Regelui Charles al III-lea din Viscri găzduiește expoziția FRAGILIS TERRA a sculptorului Virgil Scripcariu
mix_items.summary: Casa Regelui Charles al III-lea din Viscri prezintă „FRAGILIS TERRA”, un proiect artistic semnat de sculptorul Virgil Scripcariu. Expoziția, deschisă până pe 25 august 2026, este o invitație la reflecție… Articolul Casa Regelui Charles al III-lea din Viscri găzduiește expoziția FRAGILIS TERRA a sculptorului Virgil Scripcariu apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Atenţie, şoferi! Şpăgile în Bulgaria se dau de anul acesta doar în euro
mix_items.summary: Informație importantă pentru românii care merg cu mașina spre litoralul bulgăresc sau spre Grecia: nu uitați că Bulgaria a trecut la începutul anului la euro, deci șpaga către polițiști poate fi achitată doar în euro, nu și în leva, ca până acum. „Am oprit deja mai mulți turiști români care încercau să ne dea bancnote […] The post Atenţie, şoferi! Şpăgile în Bulgaria se dau de anul acesta doar în euro appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Trupul unui marinar dispărut după scufundarea remorcherului la Midia a fost găsit. Continuă misiunea de recuperare a navei
mix_items.summary: Reprezentanţii Midia Marine Terminal au anunţat, miercuri, că trupul unuia dintre marinarii dispăruţi după scufundarea remorcherului Astana în Marea Neagră a fost recuperat în timpul operaţiunii de ridicare la suprafaţă a ambarcaţiunii. Doi marinari sunt în continuare consideraţi dispăruţi în urma acestui eveniment.

mix_items.title: Lansare a volumului Émile-André Lecomte du Noüy – Cultura arhitecturală în Vechiul Regat, semnat de arh. Horia Moldovan.
mix_items.summary: Data: 19 iunie 2026 Ora: 18:00Locul: Galeria de Artă Veche Românească, Galeria Națională, etaj I, Calea Victoriei 49-53 Muzeul Național de Artă al României alături de Editura ACS vă invită vineri, 19 iunie,… Articolul Lansare a volumului Émile-André Lecomte du Noüy – Cultura arhitecturală în Vechiul Regat, semnat de arh. Horia Moldovan. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Janghina- intervenție terapeutică prin teatru în prevenirea și tratarea bullying-ului la elevi
mix_items.summary: Începând cu data de 1 iunie 2026, Asociația Academia Clar din Bacău derulează proiectul „Janghina- intervenție terapeutică prin teatru în prevenirea și tratarea bullying-ului la elevi”, derulat în cadrul Programului… Articolul Janghina- intervenție terapeutică prin teatru în prevenirea și tratarea bullying-ului la elevi apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: The post …s-au veșteajit appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Vara continuă la Roaba de Cultură cu o nouă serie de evenimente în aer liber, dedicate tuturor celor care vor să se bucure de artă, mișcare și timp petrecut în… Articolul Program evenimente Roaba de Cultură – 16-21 iunie 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Horia Mihail continuă Pianul Călător 2026
mix_items.summary: Un turneu naţional în nouă orașe, patru mari pianişti compozitori – Mozart, Beethoven, Schumann, Chopin – și două săptămâni de călătorie muzicală prin România Marți, 16 iunie, pianistul Horia Mihail… Articolul Horia Mihail continuă Pianul Călător 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Atelierul de poetică teatrală „Ion Caramitru”: zile intense de lucru, expoziție Helmut Stürmer și DEMO cu intrare liberă pentru public la Alba Iulia
mix_items.summary: UNITER – Uniunea Teatrală din România, Teatrul de Păpuși „Prichindel” Alba Iulia și Consiliul Județean Alba organizează, în perioada 10–18 iunie 2026, la Teatrul de Păpuși „Prichindel” Alba Iulia, cea… Articolul Atelierul de poetică teatrală „Ion Caramitru”: zile intense de lucru, expoziție Helmut Stürmer și DEMO cu intrare liberă pentru public la Alba Iulia apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Opera Fair: 7 zile de concerte, filme în aer liber, street food, jocuri și experiențe pentru copii și familii 22 – 28 iunie 2026, în Parcul Operei din Cluj
mix_items.summary: Muzica întâlnește joaca și învățarea în prima săptămână din vacanța mare, 22 – 28 iunie, în Parcul Operei (Cluj) În perioada 22-28 iunie, în cadrul ediției aniversare a festivalului Opera… Articolul Opera Fair: 7 zile de concerte, filme în aer liber, street food, jocuri și experiențe pentru copii și familii 22 – 28 iunie 2026, în Parcul Operei din Cluj apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: București, 1 iunie 2026. Aproape un sfert dintre adolescenții consultați de World Vision România spun că adulții îi ascultă rar... Constanţa Număr de poziții: 2 Contract: full time Grija și încrederea unei familii, emoție pozitivă, zâmbete, suport și încurajare, nevoi de bază îndeplinite și vise transformate... The post Asistent Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Colorectal cancer research reveals new mechanism in targeted therapy against metastasis
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Medical University of Vienna have described a mechanism in metastatic colorectal cancer that has received little attention to date: A key target of existing therapies, the so-called epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), is likely to affect more than just the cancer cells themselves. The study shows that EGFR also influences certain immune cells in the tumor microenvironment—and thereby helps determine how effectively the body's own defenses can combat the tumor. The findings have recently been published in the journal Cell Death & Differentiation.

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: Swiss lake symbiosis reveals unexpected role in nitrogen cycling
mix_items.summary: A publication led by researchers from the Max Planck Institute for Marine Microbiology in Bremen, Germany, shows that microscopic partnerships between ciliates and bacteria play a role in the nitrogen cycle of lakes. The study, published in The ISME Journal, investigates what determines the ecological niche of the remarkable symbiosis, and how strongly the host depends on its microbial partners.

mix_items.title: World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași.
mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... Articolul Doar 1 din 2 copii ar anunța poliția în cazul unui abuz. World Vision România: Copiii care raportează trebuie sprijiniți și încurajați, nu umiliți public. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Physical and mental capacity linked to disability before death
mix_items.summary: For many older adults, aging well means remaining independent—being able to do everyday activities like buying groceries or cooking dinner without help. A new Yale School of Medicine study suggests that changes in mental, physical or sensory capacity may be linked to a person's future ability to perform these routine tasks. The study is published in The Lancet Healthy Longevity.

mix_items.title: Contabil
mix_items.summary: Locație: București (Satul SOS) Nivel carieră: Mid-level (3-5 ani experiență) Departament: Financiar / Contabilitate Tip job: Full-time Cine suntem noi? La SOS Satele Copiilor România, oferim o familie copiilor ajunși... The post Contabil appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: 'High-res' is the secret to finding alien life with the next great space telescope
mix_items.summary: We're still in the definition phase of the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO), but it seems like every week a new research group comes out with a paper helping to shape what is becoming one of the most important space telescopes of the 2040s. A new paper posted to the arXiv preprint server from a team of researchers led by Daniel Jaffe of the University of Texas at Austin contributes to this ongoing definition work by arguing that it's time HWO adopted a high-resolution near-IR spectroscopy capability—which sounds great in practice, but so far hasn't been attempted because of technological limitations. But, according to the paper, two recent inventions finally make a working version of an extremely high-resolution exoplanet hunter viable.

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Bringing ancient light-sensing proteins back to life
mix_items.summary: Resurrecting dinosaurs using DNA retrieved from a mosquito trapped in amber is a great movie plot, though it's less likely to happen in the real world. However, researchers have been trying to unlock the secrets behind the evolution of a single protein family to understand the evolution of ancestral proteins.

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: With neuronal data, AI models predict grammar, meaning and context of spoken sentences
mix_items.summary: By applying machine-learning models to single-cell brain recordings taken from humans in conversation, a research team identified both individual and collective neuronal activity that reflected key features of language. The work, published in Nature, offers unprecedented insight into how neurons encode linguistic information, suggesting that brain activity may one day be used to infer speech-related thoughts, which could be transformative for some patients.

mix_items.title: Rewired metabolism helps revive exhausted immune cells and boost cancer immunity
mix_items.summary: Researchers from National Taiwan University (NTU) and National Taiwan University Hospital (NTUH) have identified a promising way to reinvigorate the body's cancer-fighting immune cells by rewiring their metabolism, revealing a potential new strategy for cancer immunotherapy.

mix_items.title: When health matters, status doesn't
mix_items.summary: People become much less concerned about being better off than other people when health enters the picture, according to new research.

mix_items.title: Is the customer always right? Study finds rude customers hurt business
mix_items.summary: Frontline employees who face rude or disrespectful customers are more likely to justify negative behaviors, from cutting corners to leaving their jobs, according to a new study.

mix_items.title: Reversible chirality switching in MoS₂ generates spin currents without magnets
mix_items.summary: A newly developed method allows researchers to dynamically switch chirality—a particular lack of mirror symmetry—to generate spin currents in semiconductors, researchers from Science Tokyo report. Their approach relies on the reversible insertion and removal of small chiral molecules from the interlayer gaps of a layered, nonchiral semiconductor material using electrochemistry.

mix_items.title: Neighborhood opportunities can shape children's brain development, study finds
mix_items.summary: The environment in which children grow up can play a central role in their mental development and psychological well-being. For instance, past studies have found that a family's socioeconomic status and the opportunities available to children are linked to different developmental outcomes, such as learning trajectories, attention and problem-solving capabilities.

mix_items.title: Cockroach genomes are packed with DNA transferred by their endosymbiont bacterial partners
mix_items.summary: Genes aren't just transferred from parents to their offspring. Nature has found other ways to pass on genetic information, even between different species. And a new study, published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, reports that cockroaches, in particular, are riddled with DNA transferred from another species.

mix_items.title: Skin and color pattern of 125-million-year-old crocodile revealed by extraordinary fossil from the Pyrenees
mix_items.summary: A new study published in Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society describes, for the first time in detail, the soft tissues preserved in Montsecosuchus depereti, a Lower Cretaceous crocodylomorph from the Pedrera de Meià site in the province of Lleida (Catalonia, Spain). The analysis, based on ultraviolet light, provides new clues to the coloration of these animals, as well as the evolution of the respiratory system. The research was led by the Institut Català de Paleontologia Miquel Crusafont.

mix_items.title: Global data declaration targets illegal fishing
mix_items.summary: Fifteen countries signed a global declaration Wednesday aimed at combating illegal and destructive fishing with better data.

mix_items.title: Psilocybin combined with psychotherapy demonstrates antidepressant effects over a 12-month period
mix_items.summary: Psilocybin combined with psychotherapy can lead to long-term improvements in depressive symptoms in people with treatment-resistant depression. This has been shown by a recently published long-term follow-up study conducted by the Central Institute of Mental Health (CIMH), Charité–Universitätsmedizin Berlin and the MIND Foundation. The antidepressant effects persisted for up to 12 months.

mix_items.title: Lymphatic dysfunction drives heart valve disease in Marfan syndrome patients
mix_items.summary: Dysfunction in the lymphatic system has been identified as a hidden driver of life-threatening heart valve disease in patients with Marfan syndrome, according to a new Northwestern Medicine study published in the Journal of Clinical Investigation.

mix_items.title: Premature babies—a simple test gauges neurocognitive development
mix_items.summary: Canadian scientists have demonstrated for the first time that a noninvasive brain-monitoring technique could help medical teams predict the future development of premature babies.

mix_items.title: Drug-free nanoparticles stop tumor growth by transmitting biological messages to immune cells
mix_items.summary: A research team from the Technion's Wolfson Faculty of Chemical Engineering has developed an original technology for treating cancer using nanoparticles that carry no drugs at all and has demonstrated its effectiveness against particularly dangerous and stubborn tumors.

mix_items.title: Semiconductor chip writes 64 DNA sequences in water, setting new enzymatic benchmark
mix_items.summary: Silicon chips have powered computing for half a century. Increasingly, they are also becoming platforms to read and manipulate biology at scale—recording from many neurons, reading many DNA sequences and now synthesizing DNA.

mix_items.title: AI tool outperforms existing methods for identifying severe childhood pneumonia
mix_items.summary: Pneumonia remains the leading infectious cause of death among children under 5 worldwide, claiming almost 1 million lives each year. Researchers at University College Dublin have developed an artificial intelligence tool that can more accurately identify young children with pneumonia at serious risk of needing hospital treatment. Found to significantly outperform existing risk assessment methods used to identify children who require urgent referral to the hospital, the BIOTOPE algorithm has the potential to save lives in low-resource health care settings.

mix_items.title: Infant sleep movements reveal 60-minute cycles of inactivity that lengthen through first year
mix_items.summary: Infants' activity while sleeping is rhythmic, according to a new study from the University of Surrey. The findings provide crucial insight into sleep cycles of infants in the first year of life. In the largest study of its kind, scientists from Surrey, led by Dr. Eva Winnebeck, investigated the sleep cycle development of 152 infants at ages 3, 6 and 12 months.

mix_items.title: New heat-regulating fabric feels fluffy like cotton—but doesn't get wet
mix_items.summary: Once cotton gets wet, it pulls heat from your body. This is helpful when you're exercising or outside on a hot day, but dangerous in the bitter cold. Now, researchers reporting in ACS Energy Letters have created an ultralight synthetic fiber material with cotton-like fluffiness that also repels water. The prototype fabric outperformed regular cotton in both retaining heat in the cold and releasing heat at room temperature.

mix_items.title: How early life experiences shape schizophrenia risk
mix_items.summary: Carnegie Mellon University and University of California, San Francisco researchers have found that childhood trauma, poverty, social isolation and other adverse life experiences are associated with brain changes linked to schizophrenia-spectrum disorders—findings that could help researchers identify people at risk earlier and develop interventions before severe symptoms emerge.

mix_items.title: Everyday chemical exposures linked to preterm birth and lower birthweight
mix_items.summary: In one of the largest studies of chemical exposures during pregnancy to date, new research led by the UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health, Stanford University School of Medicine and Woods Institute for the Environment has found that pregnant women are exposed to dozens of everyday chemicals, many of which can affect how early a child is born or a child's weight at birth. These outcomes influence a child's health throughout life.

mix_items.title: 800 visits to a suburban park: Inside an epic 40‑year birdwatching quest
mix_items.summary: Birdwatching is one of the most common and popular ways people experience nature. But it's not often that even the most enthusiastic birdwatcher returns to the same place more than 800 times to witness the changes over four decades.

mix_items.title: Quantum sensor overcomes major obstacle in search for dark matter and gravitational waves
mix_items.summary: A prototype quantum sensor developed by researchers at Imperial has demonstrated for the first time that a key principle behind next-generation quantum detectors can work under realistic conditions.

mix_items.title: Birth control pills may increase binge eating, new study reveals
mix_items.summary: A new study from Michigan State University found increases in binge eating when taking hormone pills in the form of oral contraceptives—but not in all women. This is the first large-scale study of changes in binge-related symptoms with oral contraceptive use.

mix_items.title: Thawing permafrost may trigger overlooked carbon sink in rivers
mix_items.summary: A new study published in Nature shows that rock weathering increasingly counteracts river CO2 emissions as permafrost degrades. The study was carried out by a collaborative team of researchers from Umeå University in Sweden and East China Normal University.

mix_items.title: Newborn neurons routinely break then repair DNA during brain cortex formation, study reveals
mix_items.summary: Newborn nerve cells must squeeze through crowded, narrow spaces—through dense tissue, past other cells, and between fibers—to reach the areas where they form neural circuits in the brain cortex. In a study published in Nature, researchers at Kyoto University's Institute for Integrated Cell-Material Sciences (WPI-iCeMS) and their collaborators report that this journey causes widespread DNA damage in neurons, resulting in double-strand breaks in which both strands of the double helix are completely severed.

mix_items.title: Bees avoid too much of a good thing by balancing nutrients in pollen, study reveals
mix_items.summary: New Oxford University-led research reveals that bees can regulate their feeding to avoid overconsuming certain essential nutrients, and that honey bees make a specialist "baby food" that gives their larvae a better-balanced diet.

mix_items.title: Parents of newborn girls are more likely to refuse lifesaving vitamin K and hepatitis B vaccine shot, researchers find
mix_items.summary: A simple shot given shortly after birth can protect babies from a rare but potentially life-threatening condition known as vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB). When newborns don't have enough vitamin K, their blood can't clot effectively, putting them at risk of severe internal bleeding in the intestines or the brain, which can cause organ damage or even prove fatal.

mix_items.title: Mountain lions have major ecological impact even in small preserves
mix_items.summary: Big cats have a big impact. A long-term study showed that when mountain lions began regular visits to a small suburban preserve about 45 miles (72 kilometers) south of San Francisco, they changed the behavior of many other animals.

mix_items.title: How do beta blocker heart drugs actually work for anxiety?
mix_items.summary: A reality TV star's claim that a beta blocker dulled their emotions and made them appear calm on camera has sparked a wave of social media commentary.

mix_items.title: Levels of 'forever chemicals' in dolphins and whales are rising globally
mix_items.summary: Whales and dolphins inhabit some of the largest and seemingly most pristine environments on Earth, from tropical coastlines to Antarctic waters. Yet even they cannot escape PFAS—persistent "forever chemicals" that leak from our homes, factories and waterways into the sea.

mix_items.title: Ozempic and Wegovy linked to surprising drop in violent behavior
mix_items.summary: A Rutgers study suggests GLP-1 drugs such as Ozempic and Wegovy may weaken the link between impulsive tendencies and violent behavior. The surprising finding hints that these medications could affect how people act on impulses, though researchers stress that cause and effect have not been proven.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover spider that disguises itself as a parasitic fungus
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered a new Amazonian spider with an astonishing disguise: it looks like a parasitic fungus. The species, Taczanowskia waska, mimics both the appearance and behavior of the fungus, helping it stay hidden from predators and potentially catch prey more easily.

mix_items.title: On the brink of extinction, the vaquita gets a digital lifeline
mix_items.summary: Scientists have digitally preserved the world’s most endangered marine mammal by creating highly detailed 3D models of a vaquita skeleton using advanced imaging technology. The virtual archive provides an unprecedented look at the species and could help inspire conservation efforts before the tiny porpoise disappears forever.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: These tiny holes could change how the world cleans water
mix_items.summary: A new nature-inspired membrane uses perfectly uniform one-nanometer pores to filter molecules with remarkable precision. The technology could transform industries such as pharmaceuticals and textiles by reducing energy consumption, improving water reuse, and delivering separation performance far beyond current filters.

mix_items.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
mix_items.summary: A low-calorie fasting-style diet significantly reduced inflammation linked to gum disease in a small clinical study. The findings suggest that what people eat may influence gum health almost as much as what they do with a toothbrush.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a surprising cancer link to Alzheimer’s disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers discovered that mutations linked to blood cancers may help trigger Alzheimer’s disease by creating overly inflammatory immune cells in the brain. The unexpected finding could lead to new blood-based screening methods and potential treatments borrowed from cancer medicine.

mix_items.title: Stanford scientists regrow lost cartilage and reverse arthritis in major breakthrough
mix_items.summary: A new treatment that blocks an aging-related protein restored lost cartilage in old mice and helped prevent arthritis after knee injuries. Human cartilage samples showed similar signs of regeneration, raising hopes for a future drug that could repair joints instead of replacing them.

mix_items.title: Giant underground neutrino detector brings scientists closer to cracking the neutrino puzzle
mix_items.summary: Deep beneath the ground in China, the massive JUNO neutrino observatory has delivered its first major scientific breakthrough, achieving one of the most precise measurements yet of how elusive neutrinos change as they travel. Using just 59 days of data, researchers sharply improved measurements of key neutrino properties, boosting confidence that JUNO can tackle one of particle physics' biggest mysteries: determining the true mass hierarchy of neutrinos.

mix_items.title: The missing notebooks that solved a 55-million-year-old fossil mystery
mix_items.summary: A spectacular fossil fish discovered on a remote cliff in New Zealand nearly 30 years ago has finally revealed its full story thanks to an unexpected discovery: the original collector’s long-lost field notebooks. The 1.2-meter fossil, preserved in stunning three-dimensional detail, belonged to an ancient tarpon-like predator that cruised New Zealand waters about 55 million years ago.

mix_items.title: A legendary golden fabric lost for 2,000 years has returned
mix_items.summary: Researchers in South Korea have recreated the legendary “sea silk” once prized by emperors, using fibers from a clam cultivated in Korean coastal waters. They discovered that its famous golden shine comes from tiny protein structures that reflect light rather than from pigments or dyes. Because the color is built into the fiber’s structure, it can remain vibrant for centuries.

mix_items.title: Ancient DNA shared with Neanderthals may explain human language
mix_items.summary: A tiny set of ancient genetic “switches” may have played a surprisingly large role in making human language possible. Researchers found that these DNA regions, which act like volume controls for genes involved in brain development, have an outsized influence on language ability despite making up less than 0.1% of the genome.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am luat sticla de apa ca sa fiu adult responsabil. Am umplut-o cu suc.

satire_ro.title: Dublă lovitură pentru Piedone jr.! După excluderea din partid, l-au dat afară şi din Mensa
